title: HTML5 Mastery
content:
# 🏗️ HTML5 Mastery & Semantic Architecture HTML (HyperText Markup Language) is the ultimate foundation of the internet. It is the invisible skeleton that gives structure to every web page, React component, and Next.js application in existence. In this module, we will start from the absolute basics of how to write an HTML tag, and scale up to professional, semantic architecture. --- ## 1️⃣ What is an HTML Element? HTML is a **Markup Language**, not a programming language. It doesn't have logic, loops, or variables. Instead, it uses **Tags** to "mark up" text so the browser knows how to display it. ### Syntax of a Tag A basic HTML element consists of an opening tag, the content, and a closing tag: ```html

This is a paragraph.

``` - `

` is the **opening tag**. - `This is a paragraph.` is the **content**. - `

` is the **closing tag** (notice the forward slash `/`). ### Paired vs. Unpaired (Void) Tags Most tags are **paired**, meaning they wrap around content like a sandwich (`

Heading

`, ``). However, some tags are **unpaired** (also called void or self-closing tags). They don't wrap content because they *are* the content itself. - `
`: Line Break (forces text to the next line). - `
`: Horizontal Rule (draws a visible dividing line). - ``: Image (embeds a picture into the page). ```html

First line of text.
Second line of text.

``` ### HTML Attributes Tags can also have **Attributes**. Attributes provide extra information about the element and are always placed inside the *opening* tag. They usually come in `name="value"` pairs. ```html Click here to search ``` --- ## 2️⃣ Text Content & Flow ### Headings (`

` through `

`) Headings are used for titles and subtitles. `

` is the most important, and `

` is the least important. **The Golden Rule:** You must have **exactly one `

`** per page for SEO. ### Block vs. Inline Elements This is a fundamental concept in web layout: 1. **Block Elements**: Start on a new line and take up the full width (e.g., `
`, `

`, `

`, `

`). 2. **Inline Elements**: Stay on the same line and only take as much width as their content (e.g., ``, ``, ``, ``). ### Advanced Text Formatting Beyond just paragraphs, we can style text semantically: - ``: Bold text (strong importance). - ``: Italic text (emphasis). - ``: Underlined text. - ``: Strikethrough text. - ``: Subscript (e.g., H2O). - ``: Superscript (e.g., E = mc2). - ``: Highlighted text. ```html

The water level is high. [1]

Important: JavaScript is mandatory.

``` --- ## 3️⃣ Organizing Data: Lists & Tables ### Lists - **Unordered List (`
    `)**: For bulleted items where order doesn't matter. - **Ordered List (`
      `)**: For numbered steps where sequence is key. - Both use **`
    1. `** (List Item) for each entry. ```html
      • Rice
      • Beans
      ``` ### Tables Tables represent structured, tabular data. - ``: The wrapper. - ``, ``, ``: Logical segments. - ``: Table Row. - `` for rows, `
      `: Table Header cell (bold/centered by default). - ``: Table Data cell. ```html
      Course Duration
      Web Foundations 4 Weeks
      ``` --- ## 4️⃣ The Document Skeleton Every HTML file needs this boilerplate: ```html My First Website

      Hello World

      ``` ### The Viewport Meta Tag This tag ensures your site looks correct on mobile devices. It scales the content to fit the screen width. --- ## 5️⃣ The Death of "Div Soup" (Semantic HTML) Instead of using generic `
      ` tags everywhere, use semantic tags to describe content: - `
      `: Site banner/navigation. - `
      `: Navigation links. - `
      `: Central content unique to the page. - `
      `: Self-contained content (blog post). - `
      `: Thematic grouping. - `
      `: Copyright/links at bottom. --- ## 6️⃣ Forms & Inputs ### Control Selection 1. **Radio Buttons**: Pick **exactly one** (e.g., Gender). 2. **Checkboxes**: Pick **zero or more** (e.g., Skills). 3. **Select (Dropdown)**: Pick one from a **long list** (e.g., Country). 4. **Textarea**: For **long-form** text (e.g., Message). ### Form Example ```html

      Pick one color:

      ``` --- ## 💡 Summary | Concept | Key Takeaway | |---|---| | **Block vs Inline** | Block stacks vertically; Inline sits horizontally side-by-side. | | **Formatting** | Use ``, ``, `` etc. for semantic text styling. | | **Lists** | `
        ` for bullets, `
          ` for numbers. | | **Tables** | `
      ` for data, `` for headers. | | **Forms** | Radios are for single choices; Checkboxes for multiple. |

questions.question_text: Which HTML5 element is used to specify a footer for a document or section?
questions.explanation: The <footer> element is a semantic tag used to define a footer for its nearest sectioning content.

questions.question_text: What does the <main> tag represent in an HTML document?
questions.explanation: The <main> element should contain content that is unique to that document and not repeated across pages.

questions.question_text: Which attribute is used to provide alternative text for an image if it cannot be displayed?
questions.explanation: The 'alt' attribute is crucial for accessibility (screen readers) and SEO.

questions.question_text: What is the purpose of the <nav> element?
options: To define a set of navigation links
To link to external websites only
questions.explanation: The <nav> element is intended for major blocks of navigation links.

questions.question_text: Which tag is used to define an unordered list in HTML5?
questions.explanation: <ul> stands for Unordered List, typically displayed with bullet points.

questions.question_text: What is the main difference between block and inline elements?
options: Block elements are faster to load.
Inline elements always start on a new line.
Block elements take up the full width available; inline elements do not.
questions.explanation:
Block elements (like
or

) stack vertically, while inline elements (like or ) sit side-by-side.

questions.question_text: Which tag is used to create a superscript, like the '2' in E=mc²?
questions.explanation: <sup> stands for superscript. <sub> is for subscript.

questions.question_text: In a table, which tag represents a standard data cell?
questions.explanation: <td> stands for Table Data. <th> is for Table Header.

questions.question_text: Which attribute on a form specifies where to send the data?
questions.explanation: The 'action' attribute defines the URL where the form data will be sent upon submission.

questions.question_text: When should you use a Radio button instead of a Checkbox?
options: When you want the user to pick multiple items.
When you want the user to pick exactly one item from a list.
When you have a very long list of items.
Radio buttons are only for numbers.
questions.explanation: Radio buttons are for mutually exclusive choices (pick one). Checkboxes are for multiple choices.

questions.question_text: What is the purpose of the <hr> tag?
options: To draw a horizontal rule (thematic break).
questions.explanation: The <hr> tag inserts a horizontal line to separate content sections.
